docs.title: Welcome to MkDocs
docs.text: For full documentation visit mkdocs.org.

docs.title: Commands
docs.text:
• mkdocs new [dir-name] - Create a new project.
  • mkdocs serve - Start the live-reloading docs server.
  • mkdocs build - Build the documentation site.
  • mkdocs -h - Print help message and exit.

docs.title: Project layout
docs.text:
mkdocs.yml    # The configuration file.
docs/
    index.md  # The documentation homepage.
    ...       # Other markdown pages, images and other files.

docs.title: Code Blocks
docs.text: Material for MkDocs renders beautiful, feature-rich code blocks out of the box. With a few extensions enabled you get syntax highlighting, line numbers, annotations, and copy buttons.

docs.title: Configuration
docs.text:
theme:
  features:
    - content.code.copy # adds a copy button to every block
    - content.code.annotate # enables inline annotations

markdown_extensions:
  - pymdownx.highlight:
      anchor_linenums: true
      line_spans: __span
      pygments_lang_class: true
  - pymdownx.inlinehilite
  - pymdownx.superfences

docs.title: Basic Fenced Code Block
docs.text:
Wrap code in triple backticks and add a language identifier for syntax highlighting. Pygments supports 500+ languages.

```python
def greet(name: str) -> str:
    return f"Hello, {name}!"

print(greet("world"))
```

Result:

def greet(name: str) -> str:
    return f"Hello, {name}!"

print(greet("world"))

docs.title: Adding a Title
docs.text:
Use the title attribute to label your code block — useful for showing a filename.

```yaml title="mkdocs.yml"
site_name: Su and Ivy's docs
theme:
  name: material
```

Result:

mkdocs.yml
site_name: Su and Ivy's docs
theme:
  name: material

docs.title: Line Numbers
docs.text:
Add linenums="1" to start numbering from line 1 (or any other number).

```python linenums="1"
import httpx

response = httpx.get("https://api.example.com/v1/users")
print(response.json())
```

Result:

import httpx

response = httpx.get("https://api.example.com/v1/users")
print(response.json())

docs.title: Highlighting Lines
docs.text:
Use hl_lines to draw attention to specific lines.

```python linenums="1" hl_lines="2 3"
def connect(host: str, port: int):
    if port not in (80, 443):
        raise ValueError(f"Unsupported port: {port}")
    return f"Connected to {host}:{port}"
```

Result:

def connect(host: str, port: int):
    if port not in (80, 443):
        raise ValueError(f"Unsupported port: {port}")
    return f"Connected to {host}:{port}"

docs.title: Inline Code Highlighting
docs.text:
With pymdownx.inlinehilite you can highlight inline snippets:

Use `#!python print("hello")` to output text, or `#!bash export KEY=value` to set env vars.

Result: Use print("hello") to output text, or export KEY=value to set env vars.

docs.title: Code Annotations
docs.text:
Annotations let you attach explanatory notes to specific lines. Add # (1) markers in the code, then list the explanations below with 1..

```yaml
theme:
  name: material
  features:
    - content.code.copy # (1)!
```

1. Adds a one-click copy button to every code block on the page.

Result:

theme:
  name: material
  features:
    - content.code.copy # (1)!
  1. Adds a one-click copy button to every code block on the page.

docs.title: Content Tabs
docs.text: Content tabs let you group related content under selectable labels — ideal for showing the same instructions in multiple languages, or comparing alternative approaches side by side.

docs.title: Configuration
docs.text:
markdown_extensions:
  - pymdownx.tabbed:
      alternate_style: true # required for the modern tab style
  - pymdownx.superfences # needed for code blocks inside tabs

docs.title: Basic Tabs
docs.text:
Wrap tab content with === "Label" blocks. Indentation must be consistent (4 spaces).

=== "Python"
`python
    import httpx
    response = httpx.get("https://api.example.com/v1/users")
    `

=== "JavaScript"
`js
    const res = await fetch("https://api.example.com/v1/users");
    const data = await res.json();
    `

=== "cURL"
`bash
    curl https://api.example.com/v1/users \
      -H "Authorization: Bearer $TOKEN"
    `

Result:

Python

python import httpx response = httpx.get("https://api.example.com/v1/users")

JavaScript

js const res = await fetch("https://api.example.com/v1/users"); const data = await res.json();

cURL

bash curl https://api.example.com/v1/users \ -H "Authorization: Bearer $TOKEN"

docs.title: Tabs with Text and Lists
docs.text:
Tab content isn't limited to code — you can include any Markdown inside a tab.

=== ":material-apple: macOS"
Install via Homebrew:

    1. Open Terminal
    2. Run `brew install mkdocs`
    3. Verify with `mkdocs --version`

=== ":material-linux: Linux"
Install via pip:

    1. Ensure Python 3.8+ is installed
    2. Run `pip install mkdocs-material`
    3. Verify with `mkdocs --version`

=== ":material-microsoft-windows: Windows"
Install via pip in PowerShell:

    1. Ensure Python 3.8+ is installed
    2. Run `pip install mkdocs-material`
    3. Verify with `mkdocs --version`

Result:

:material-apple: macOS

Install via Homebrew:

1. Open Terminal
2. Run `brew install mkdocs`
3. Verify with `mkdocs --version`
:material-linux: Linux

Install via pip:

1. Ensure Python 3.8+ is installed
2. Run `pip install mkdocs-material`
3. Verify with `mkdocs --version`
:material-microsoft-windows: Windows

Install via pip in PowerShell:

1. Ensure Python 3.8+ is installed
2. Run `pip install mkdocs-material`
3. Verify with `mkdocs --version`

docs.title: Linked Tabs
docs.text:
By default, all tab groups on a page switch independently. To sync tabs with the same label across the whole page, add slugify to your config:

markdown_extensions:
  - pymdownx.tabbed:
      alternate_style: true
      slugify: !!python/object/apply:pymdownx.slugs.slugify
        kwds:
          case: lower

When a reader selects "Python" in one group, every other tab group on the page that has a "Python" tab will switch to it automatically.

docs.title: Setting up Emojis
docs.text: Material for MkDocs supports the full set of Twemoji and Material Design Icons via a simple shortcode syntax. Once configured, you can drop emojis and icons anywhere in your Markdown.

docs.title: Configuration
docs.text:
Add the following to your mkdocs.yml:

markdown_extensions:
  - attr_list
  - pymdownx.emoji:
      emoji_index: !!python/name:material.extensions.emoji.twemoji
      emoji_generator: !!python/name:material.extensions.emoji.to_svg

docs.title: Basic Usage
docs.text:
Use :name: shortcodes inline with your text:

:smile: :rocket: :warning: :white_check_mark: :tada:

Result: :smile: :rocket: :warning: :white_check_mark: :tada:

docs.title: Common Emoji Shortcodes
docs.text: Shortcode Emoji Use case :rocket: 🚀 Launches, deploys :white_check_mark: ✅ Success, completed :warning: ⚠️ Caution, deprecation :x: ❌ Errors, unsupported :bulb: 💡 Tips, ideas :book: 📖 Documentation, reading :lock: 🔒 Security, auth :gear: ⚙️ Configuration, settings

docs.title: Emoji in Headings and Lists
docs.text:
Emojis work inside headings, list items, and tables — anywhere Markdown is rendered.

## :rocket: Deployment

- :white_check_mark: Step one complete
- :gear: Configuring environment
- :lock: Securing credentials

Result:

docs.title: :rocket: Deployment
docs.text:
• :white_check_mark: Step one complete
  • :gear: Configuring environment
  • :lock: Securing credentials

docs.title: Finding Emojis
docs.text: Browse the full list at emojipedia.org or search the Twemoji cheat sheet. Most standard emoji names work as-is with underscores replacing spaces.

docs.title: Diagrams
docs.text: Material for MkDocs renders Mermaid.js diagrams natively. Write diagrams as fenced code blocks — no plugins or image exports needed.

docs.title: Configuration
docs.text:
markdown_extensions:
  - pymdownx.superfences:
      custom_fences:
        - name: mermaid
          class: mermaid
          format: !!python/name:pymdownx.superfences.fence_code_format

docs.title: Flowchart
docs.text:
Use flowcharts to illustrate processes, decision trees, and request lifecycles.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
  A[Client] --> B{Auth?}
  B -- Yes --> C[API Server]
  B -- No  --> D[401 Unauthorized]
  C --> E[(Database)]
  C --> F[200 Response]
```

Result:

flowchart LR
  A[Client] --> B{Auth?}
  B -- Yes --> C[API Server]
  B -- No  --> D[401 Unauthorized]
  C --> E[(Database)]
  C --> F[200 Response]

docs.title: Sequence Diagram
docs.text:
Sequence diagrams show message exchanges between actors over time — perfect for documenting authentication flows and API handshakes.

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
  autonumber
  participant C as Client
  participant A as Auth Service
  participant S as API Server

  C->>A: POST /oauth/token
  A-->>C: access_token
  C->>S: GET /v1/data (Bearer token)
  S->>A: Validate token
  A-->>S: Token valid
  S-->>C: 200 OK { data }
```

Result:

sequenceDiagram
  autonumber
  participant C as Client
  participant A as Auth Service
  participant S as API Server

  C->>A: POST /oauth/token
  A-->>C: access_token
  C->>S: GET /v1/data (Bearer token)
  S->>A: Validate token
  A-->>S: Token valid
  S-->>C: 200 OK { data }

docs.title: Entity Relationship Diagram
docs.text:
Use ERDs to document data models and database schemas.

```mermaid
erDiagram
  USER {
    uuid   id PK
    string email
    string name
  }
  COLLECTION {
    uuid   id PK
    uuid   owner_id FK
    string name
  }
  REQUEST {
    uuid   id PK
    uuid   collection_id FK
    string method
    string url
  }

  USER        ||--o{ COLLECTION : owns
  COLLECTION  ||--o{ REQUEST    : contains
```

Result:

erDiagram
  USER {
    uuid   id PK
    string email
    string name
  }
  COLLECTION {
    uuid   id PK
    uuid   owner_id FK
    string name
  }
  REQUEST {
    uuid   id PK
    uuid   collection_id FK
    string method
    string url
  }

  USER        ||--o{ COLLECTION : owns
  COLLECTION  ||--o{ REQUEST    : contains

docs.title: State Diagram
docs.text:
State diagrams are useful for documenting lifecycle states — webhooks, job queues, order statuses, and more.

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
  [*]      --> Pending
  Pending  --> Running  : job starts
  Running  --> Success  : completes
  Running  --> Failed   : error
  Failed   --> Pending  : retry
  Success  --> [*]
```

Result:

stateDiagram-v2
  [*]      --> Pending
  Pending  --> Running  : job starts
  Running  --> Success  : completes
  Running  --> Failed   : error
  Failed   --> Pending  : retry
  Success  --> [*]

docs.title: Tips
docs.text:
• Mermaid diagrams adapt automatically to light and dark mode.
  • Keep diagrams small and focused — one concept per diagram is easier to read.
  • For complex architecture diagrams, consider splitting into multiple focused diagrams rather than one large one.
  • Full Mermaid syntax reference: mermaid.js.org
